title: Coalescence of geodesics and the BKS midpoint problem in planar first-passage percolation
speaker: Ron Peled
affiliation: Tel Aviv University, Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: First-passage percolation studies the geometry obtained from a random perturbation of Euclidean geometry. In the discrete planar setting, one assigns random, independent and identically distributed, lengths to the edges of the lattice Z^2 and studies the resulting geodesics - paths of minimal length between points. While the physics literature presents an elaborate picture for the behavior of the model, placing it in the KPZ universality class, mathematical progress remains rather limited.

proof_stages.label: Planar FPP metric and KPZ fluctuation framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines planar first-passage percolation as an i.i.d. edge-weight random metric on Z^2, introduces unique geodesics, and reviews the KPZ predictions for the limit shape, passage-time fluctuations, and transversal fluctuations together with the large gap between predictions and known results.

proof_stages.label: BKS midpoint problem from bi-infinite-geodesic motivation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker motivates bi-infinite geodesics via FPP growth and disordered Ising domain walls, then formulates the BKS midpoint problem as the expected delocalization of a long geodesic away from the midpoint and recalls prior nonquantitative solutions.

proof_stages.label: Limit-shape assumption and coalescence theorem
proof_stages.summary: Under the assumption that the limit-shape unit ball has at least 32 sides, verified for certain perturbative edge-weight laws, the main theorem states that geodesics whose endpoints are moved within l^alpha boxes for any alpha<1/8 coalesce with polynomially high probability, up to a small symmetric-difference error.

proof_stages.label: Deduction of a quantitative BKS midpoint bound
proof_stages.summary: The coalescence theorem implies a polynomial upper bound on the probability of hitting the midpoint, because otherwise translating both endpoints through a box would force too many nearby translated geodesics to pass through one point and hence force the original geodesic to spend area-order length in a box, contradicting a shortcut bound.

proof_stages.label: Trap reduction using upper and lower comparison geodesics
proof_stages.summary: To prove coalescence, the argument constructs upper and lower geodesics from endpoints displaced by about l^(1/8); if these comparison geodesics intersect, then planar trapping plus the limit-shape assumption prevents intermediate geodesics from going around and forces coalescence.

proof_stages.label: Attractive geodesic mechanism
proof_stages.summary: A key proposition says that two geodesics spending most of their time near each other must intersect; its proof decomposes one geodesic into patches and uses an FKG-type conditional comparison showing the unused environment above it is stochastically heavier, creating shortcut opportunities for the other geodesic.

proof_stages.label: Planar ordering and Markov control of separation
proof_stages.summary: Planarity and ordering keep the comparison geodesics close: translation invariance gives expected cross-section separation comparable to their initial separation r, and Markov inequalities make large separations unlikely on most cross-sections, providing the hypothesis needed for the attractive-geodesic proposition.

examples.name: Brownian and straight-line diffusive benchmarks
examples.note: Brownian motion with transversal exponent 1/2 and the variance of summing weights along a fixed line give familiar diffusive comparisons for the KPZ exponents in FPP.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes benchmark fluctuation orders/exponents such as Brownian transversal scale sqrt(l) giving xi=1/2 and straight-line sums having standard deviation of order sqrt(l)

passes.reason: Brownian motion and straight lines serve as highly regular diffusive/geodesic baseline cases rather than refutations.

examples.name: The l-infinity limit-shape ball
examples.note: The l-infinity norm ball illustrates why lack of strict curvature could allow linear-scale detours and why some limit-shape control is needed to prevent geodesics from escaping a trap.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through geodesics in the l-infinity ball, explaining how a path can deviate linearly in height with little loss and using it to contrast strict convexity

passes.reason: The l-infinity ball is a regular square with the minimal flat-sided shape, used as the enemy case where non-strict convexity permits linear wandering.
The l-infinity ball is the square endpoint of the Lp norms with flat sides and high lattice symmetry, used as the foil where linear detours can remain geodesic.
The l-infinity ball is a highly symmetric non-strictly-convex endpoint/minimal-four-sided shape used as the enemy case allowing linear wandering.
The l-infinity ball is the highly symmetric square endpoint of norm balls and is used as the adversarial non-strictly-convex shape allowing linear geodesic wandering.
The l-infinity ball is a highly symmetric square and an endpoint norm ball, used as the flat-sided obstruction allowing linear geodesic detours.
The square l-infinity ball is a flat-sided, highly symmetric limiting norm ball allowing maximal linear deviations, used as the enemy case to strict-convexity/sublinear-wandering intuition.
The l-infinity ball is used as a hypothetical obstruction to sublinear geodesic wandering; structurally it is a highly symmetric square with flat sides and the minimal four extreme points.
The l-infinity ball is a regular square norm ball with the minimum four sides and flat facets, used as the enemy case where geodesics can wander linearly.
The square l-infinity ball is a maximally symmetric minimal-sided norm ball used to show how non-strict convexity would permit linear geodesic wandering.
Serves as the flat-sided enemy shape where geodesics can wander linearly; the square l-infinity ball is highly symmetric and has the minimal four extreme points.
The square l-infinity ball is a maximally coordinate-symmetric flat-sided limiting norm used as the enemy case where linear wandering could occur.
Used as a flat-sided obstruction showing why lack of strict convexity could allow linear geodesic wandering; structurally it is the highly symmetric endpoint ℓ∞ square ball.

examples.name: Exactly solvable directed last-passage models
examples.note: Directed last-passage percolation, especially with exponential weights, is used as a comparison class where limit shapes, exponents, absence of bi-geodesics, and coalescence are much better understood.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites directed last-passage models as exactly solvable cases with known formulas and a parabolic norm but does not derive or compute them

passes.reason: Exactly solvable directed LPP models are special integrable families used for comparison, not sporadic exceptions or failures of a claim.

examples.name: Higher-dimensional FPP
examples.note: Questions about three and higher dimensions highlight that exponent predictions and especially coalescence of one-dimensional geodesics are much less clear without planar ordering.

examples.name: Disordered Ising ferromagnet domain walls
examples.note: The random-coupling Ising ground-state interface produces the bi-infinite-geodesic viewpoint and motivates the BKS midpoint obstruction via boundary-condition domain walls.
examples.engagement_reason: he defines the disordered Ising Hamiltonian, checks constant ground states, rules out finite islands by flipping spins, and derives the connection between domain walls and bi-infinite geodesics

passes.reason: Disordered Ising ferromagnet domain walls are used to motivate the bi-geodesic question, not as known exceptional objects or counterexamples.
Ground-state domain walls are energy-minimizing interfaces, equivalently geodesic objects in the associated passage metric.
Ground-state Ising domain walls are locally energy-minimizing interfaces equivalent to bi-infinite geodesics, used as motivation rather than a counterexample.
A ground-state Ising domain wall would be an energy-minimizing geodesic interface, introduced as an equivalent perspective rather than an actual counterexample.

Ground-state Ising domain walls are energy-minimizing geodesic interfaces, used to reformulate bi-geodesic questions rather than refute them.

examples.note: Edge weights of the form one plus a small random perturbation provide a concrete class for which the authors can verify their limit-shape side assumption.
